topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara leaned back in her worn office chair, a slow, involuntary exhale escaping her lips. Her gaze drifted from the glowing screen to the physical blueprints spread across her desk. They were identical. Every line, every annotation, the precise shade of the ink—a perfect mirror image of the schematics she’d spent the last three weeks drafting. A cold knot tightened in her stomach, quickly followed by a dizzying sense of disorientation. She blinked hard, rubbing her eyes as if to clear a smudge, but the duplication remained, undeniable and utterly baffling. "Impossible," she whispered, the sound thin in the quiet room. She reached out, her fingers trembling slightly as they traced the contour of a particular circuit diagram, feeling the texture of the paper beneath her touch.

[story 2]

Mark stopped mid-stride, the coffee cup halfway to his mouth. He stared at the prototype resting on the workbench. It was the same brushed aluminum casing, the same intricate wiring layout, the same minuscule etching on the side panel. A sharp intake of breath hitched in his chest. His hands, usually steady when soldering, began to shake, causing the tools in his grip to rattle faintly. He took a hesitant step backward, bumping against a stack of manuals. "What in the world?" he managed, his voice a reedy question. He circled the object slowly, his eyes darting between the physical item and the digital file open on his monitor, a profound sense of unreality washing over him.

[story 3]

"No. No, this can't be right," whispered Lena, her fingers flying across the keyboard before freezing mid-keystroke. She looked up, her eyes wide and unblinking, fixed on the model sitting on the pedestal in the center of the lab. The holographic projection shimmering above it displayed the exact same three-dimensional rendering she had finalized just yesterday. A strange, hollow feeling settled in her chest, making her movements sluggish. She slowly lowered her hands, letting them fall to her sides. "I need to check the version control," she murmured, though the certainty of the duplication settled deep within her, a heavy, undeniable weight.
